heroText: This lesson teaches the short lines families use around serving food, calling people to eat, asking for more, and saying enough politely.
panelList: How to respond when someone calls you to eat
How to ask for more food or say enough naturally
How to make simple dinner-table requests
How to sound warm instead of stiff at home
title: At the dinner table
description: The goal here is not formal food vocabulary. The goal is being able to move through a real family meal in Tamil: come to the table, ask for a little more, say the food is good, and handle small back-and-forths naturally.

audio.note: This localhost lesson is wired for ElevenLabs-generated audio. Configure ELEVENLABS_API_KEY and ELEVENLABS_VOICE_ID in .env.local, then run serve.py.

phrases.useCase: A parent, sibling, or close family member may say this when food is ready.
phrases.note: Short invitations like this are everywhere in home Tamil. They sound normal, not abrupt.

phrases.useCase: Use this when someone calls you to the table and you want to answer quickly.
phrases.note: This is one of the highest-value short replies in home Tamil.

phrases.useCase: Useful when an elder or host is serving you food.
phrases.note: This is a gentle, very reusable dinner-table request.

phrases.useCase: Use this when you want more food without sounding demanding.
phrases.note: This is a nice way to practice the spoken `venum` pattern in a real situation.

phrases.useCase: Say this when someone is serving you and you want them to stop.
phrases.note: A tiny phrase, but extremely useful at home and when visiting people.

phrases.meaning: Please give me a little rasam.
phrases.useCase: A specific food request that also teaches a broader request pattern.
phrases.note: You can later swap `rasam` for many other food items and reuse the structure.

phrases.useCase: Use this to compliment a dish at home.
phrases.note: Appreciation matters a lot in family settings, and this line is easy to use often.

phrases.useCase: A gentle way to comment on the food without sounding rude.
phrases.note: This is more natural than a blunt complaint and keeps the tone soft.

phrases.useCase: Use this if someone offers you food after you already ate outside.
phrases.note: Code-mixing with `already` is common in urban bilingual Tamil.

wordBreakdown.meaning: keep it / set it aside please

phrases.meaning: Keep it for me, I'll eat later.
phrases.useCase: Useful when food is ready but you are not eating immediately.
phrases.note: This is a realistic family line and quietly introduces near-future meaning.

phrases.useCase: A common question you may hear while someone is serving food.
phrases.note: This helps learners recognize what is being asked without needing every word explained in the moment.

dialogues.context: You are finishing one quick thing and want to reply naturally.
dialogues.explanation: This is the most natural immediate home reply when someone calls you to eat.

dialogues.prompt: An elder is serving rice and you want a little more.
dialogues.context: Which line sounds clear and polite?
dialogues.explanation: This is the best direct request when someone is actively serving you.

dialogues.context: You are full and want them to stop serving.
dialogues.explanation: This is the cleanest short way to say enough at the table.

dialogues.prompt: You ate outside already, but someone is offering dinner at home.
dialogues.context: Which answer explains that most directly?
dialogues.explanation: This is a realistic home reply when food is offered after you already ate.

recalls.note: A very common family call when food is ready.
Use the respectful serving request at the table.
Short, practical, and very high value.
A realistic line when someone offers food after you ate outside.
